Welcome back. It's time to get into the really specific stuff here and talking about the actual step-by-step strategies that allow you to achieve your vision. The buzzword here is goals. Now, you've heard me speak about goals many times. I'm a believer in setting them. I know they have a lot of power. You've probably also heard me say that they're mostly misused. People don't know how to set goals correctly. In this interview question, she extracts out of me one of the best explanations I've ever given of how you actually create goals, how you set them, and how you're used to ensure your long-term success in terms of reaching your vision. So, let's cut right to it now and I will see you on the other side. Jordan, much of the development literature talks about creating goals or a set of goals. To you, what's the difference between the vision and the goals? And how do you use the set of goals to achieve that vision? Okay, well, goals to me are your stopping points along the way that tell you whether or not you're moving in the right direction towards your vision, you know, or you're essentially moving in the wrong direction towards your vision. So, you have your vision, which is on top of everything. It's like this higher level function. It's this higher level target that you have, which really represents you. It encapsulates your beliefs, your desires, your wants, your hope for your future at this very high level. And it's not really a date on it. It's sort of just where you want to end up in your life. As opposed to a goal, which has a start date and an end date. You know, I set a goal today and it should be achieved by, you know, June 31st, 2013. Right? That's a goal. It has an end date. A vision doesn't. That's number one. Number two, I don't become emotionally invested in my goals, but I do become emotionally invested in my vision. To me, if I think about my vision and I read my vision to myself, if I don't get to that spot where I am almost, I feel my pulse beating through my, through my wrists and the hairs in the back of my neck stand out. If I don't get to that point of excitability in that point where I feel empowered and motivated, and my vision is not really the vision that I need to get what I want in life. I need to redo that vision to get me to that sort of point where I'm excited and pumped and motivated. Right? When I look at my goals, I don't get motivated like that. I'm not looking to get motivated. When I look at my goals, I'm looking for strategy of like maybe managing time and how much effort, what are the, essentially what people don't need to meet to help me achieve this goal. What processes do I have to learn if there are any processes? What bodies of knowledge do I need to familiarize myself with to achieve a goal? A goal is sort of a very, it's almost a logical point that you're moving towards while the vision's more of an emotional point you're moving towards. I think that the two together are incredibly powerful. So I'm not, you know, back in the day of, you know, we had this amazing, actually it was a recording back then in the 60s, it was by a man named Earl Langill. And it was called The Strangest Secret. And basically at the heart of the book was this idea, which back then was revolutionary, is that you become what you think about, that you can visualize where you want to go and you can essentially manifest that in your life back then that was groundbreaking. And at the heart of Earl Nightingale's formula was this sort of goal-setting mentality to set goals, right, and then to move towards those goals and have them as your targets. And they use this study, which turned out to not be true, by the way, that, and they had taken some study at Yale University and the 5% of the people at Yale in the graduating class of 1940 who set goals ended up making more money than the other 95% of Yale who didn't set goals. It turned out not to be true, but that was a famous study for how it turned out to be, you know, false. The point was this though, and it is true, is that people who do set goals will tend to be much more successful in people who don't set goals strictly because a goal has a target and, you know, it's very hard to almost hit something that you can't see. You know, you're not a target in your life, good luck trying to get somewhere. You know, you're something like a random preacher. What happened was is that in the 1960s, that was earth shattering stuff, you know, and people would say, I'm goal oriented, you know, I'm going to set goals and it really worked. Over the years, it became bastardized. This idea of goal setting became so commonplace that, you know, everyone became goal oriented and, you know, if you look at anyone's resume, I'm a goal oriented individual, blah, blah, blah, and it means nothing. You know, you know, how many times have we all set goals and not achieved them? Everyone's raised their hand. We almost become conditioned not to achieve our goals. So the power that the strangest secret used to have, which was immense and based on goals, has now lost its power because we have essentially bastardized the word goals. Now it's about vision. It's transcended to that next level up, which means you have to have your vision. That's what empowers you. The goals now are the stopping point, essentially the map along the way, the sign that says I'm heading towards the destination, right? Whether I achieve it or not, it's not going to dictate whether I'm happy or sad in life. It just tells me if I'm heading in the right direction. If I'm heading towards my vision, I'll feel some pleasure out of it. If I feel I'm heading away from my vision, I'll feel distress and that causes me to want to work harder and to figure out a new strategy. But I'm always constantly setting goals, abandoning goals that haven't worked, resetting one, and if a goal works, I move to the next goal. But the vision is that sort of that shining light that stands above it all. Great stuff, right? It's awesome stuff. It really is. A big believer in setting goals that act as directional finders on the way to my vision. Trust me, once you start doing this, you will see the power and also how great it makes you feel when you're not emotionally invested in your goals, but they provide you with the sort of roadmap, the stepping stones, the signpost that keep you going in the direction of your vision until you ultimately get there. See you in the next lesson. Do your quiz first, then come back. Take care.
